title: 21 — The Dignity of the Human Person
html:
Big Question

What makes a person valuable? Where does your dignity come from?

Once there was a girl named Sophie who was born with Down syndrome. Her parents received «condolences» instead of congratulations. The doctors told them their daughter «would never be normal.» Friends whispered: «What a tragedy.» Twenty years later, Sophie works at a coffee shop, knows every customer by name, remembers what coffee each one prefers, and fills the place with such contagious joy that people go there not for the coffee but for her. On her twentieth birthday, someone asked her mom: «What was it like raising a daughter with Down syndrome?» Her mother replied: «At first I thought it would be a burden. Now I know it was a gift. Sophie taught me something I had never understood: that a person's worth comes not from what they can do, but from who they are.»

That is the question at the heart of this lesson: what makes a person valuable? The world answers: success, intelligence, beauty, productivity, money. But God answers in an entirely different way: every person has infinite dignity because they were created in the image of God. Before you did anything — before you were even born — you already had a value that no one can take away from you. It is not something you earn: it is something you are. It does not depend on your usefulness: it depends on your divine origin.

The Catechism opens the section on morality with this truth, because the whole of Christian morality is built upon it. Not understanding our dignity is like trying to read a map without knowing where you are standing. And understanding it is the first step toward living as what we truly are: children of God, created by love and for love (CCC 1700).

Activity

Imagine you have a $100 bill. If you crumple it, step on it, stain it… does its value change? It is still worth $100. That is what human dignity is like. No matter how much life crumples you, stains you, or tears you, your value does not change. Why? Because your value is not set by the world: it was set by God when he created you in his image.

Draw a banknote and write on it the words: «Image of God.» Then around it, write the things the world says give a person value (money, beauty, success, likes…). Ask yourself: what happens to those things when we grow old or fall ill? And what happens to being the image of God?

1. Imago Dei: Created in the Image and Likeness of God

The foundation of human dignity is found in the very first chapter of the Bible: «God created man in his own image; in the image of God he created him; male and female he created them» (Gen 1:27). This affirmation, which the Catechism calls «the basis of all Christian anthropology» (CCC 1701), radically distinguishes the human being from the rest of creation. We are not just another animal, not meaningless stardust, not a cosmic accident. We are unique creatures, willed by God from all eternity, bearers of his image.

But what exactly does it mean to be made «in the image of God»? It does not mean God has a human body (God is pure spirit). Nor does it mean we are «little gods.» It means three fundamental things (CCC 1701-1705):

First: intelligence. We are capable of knowing the truth. Not just practical truths (where to find food), but the deepest truths: Who am I? What is the meaning of my life? Does God exist? A dog does not wonder about the meaning of its existence. A computer can process data but cannot understand. Only the human being can seek the truth and find it, and in that search we reflect God, who is Truth itself.

Second: free will. We are capable of choosing the good. We do not act solely by instinct, like animals. We can say yes or no, we can love or reject, we can build or destroy. Freedom is a participation in God's creative power: like him, we can shape our life and our world through free choices. This freedom carries an immense responsibility, but also a profound dignity: only free beings can truly love.

Third: the capacity to love and enter into communion. God is Trinity — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, an eternal communion of love. The human being, created in the image of a God who is Love, is made to love. That is why loneliness hurts us so deeply: it is not a system malfunction; it is a sign that we were created for communion — with God and with others. Genesis tells it this way: «It is not good that the man should be alone» (Gen 2:18). The need for love is not weakness; it is our deepest vocation.

The Catechism sums up this threefold dimension in paragraph 1703: the human person, endowed with a spiritual soul, intelligence, and will, is from the moment of conception ordered to God and destined for eternal beatitude. We are not beings made for death: we are beings made for eternal life.

Body and Soul: An Indivisible Unity

The Catechism insists that the human being is a unity of body and soul (CCC 1705-1706). We are not a soul trapped in a body (as the ancient Greeks thought), nor are we just a body without a soul (as modern materialism thinks). We are both, in an inseparable unity. The Second Vatican Council puts it this way: «Man, body and soul, is a unity. In his bodily condition he himself sums up the elements of the material world» (Gaudium et Spes 14).

This has enormous practical consequences: the body is not a disposable object, an accessory to the person, or something shameful. It is part of who we are. That is why the Church defends the dignity of the human body: what we do with it is not morally neutral, because what affects the body affects the whole person. And that is why Christianity professes the resurrection of the flesh: not only the soul is saved, but God will recreate our body in eternal life.

Context

Maybe you are reading this and thinking: «I don't feel like I have dignity.» Or: «I've done things that make me feel worthless.» Listen carefully: dignity does not depend on how you feel. It is not self-esteem. Self-esteem rises and falls with our success, our relationships, our emotions. Dignity is something else: it is the objective value you have by virtue of who you are, regardless of what you feel. God does not love you because you are good or successful. He loves you because you are his. And that unconditional love is the rock on which any wounded self-esteem can be rebuilt.

St. Josephine Bakhita, born in Sudan around 1869, was kidnapped at the age of seven and sold into slavery. She passed through the hands of five masters who tortured her brutally: they made 114 incisions with a knife on her belly, arms, and chest, and rubbed them with salt so the scars would be permanent. They treated her as if she were not a person, as if her body had no dignity. But years later, when she encountered Christ and was freed, she became a Canossian sister and said: «If I were to meet those slave traders again, I would kneel down to kiss their hands, because if I had not gone through all that, I would never have known Christ.» No one could take away her dignity, because her dignity did not depend on her captors: it depended on God. St. Josephine Bakhita was canonized in the year 2000. She is the patron saint of victims of human trafficking.

2. Christ Reveals Man to Himself

The Second Vatican Council, in the constitution Gaudium et Spes number 22, uttered one of the most luminous phrases of the modern Magisterium: «Christ, the new Adam, fully reveals man to man himself and makes his supreme calling clear.» What does this mean? It means that if you want to know who you really are, look at Jesus Christ. Do not look at the mirrors the world holds up to you (social media, advertising, other people's expectations). Look at Christ. He is the perfect man, the perfect image of God (Col 1:15), and in him we see what we are called to become (CCC 1701-1702).

Original sin darkened the image of God in us but did not destroy it (CCC 1707-1708). It is like a mirror covered in dust: the reflection is still there, but it cannot be seen clearly. Christ came to wipe that mirror clean. In the Incarnation, God took on human nature and restored it from within. In the Redemption, he gave us back the capacity to live as children of God. And in the Resurrection, he opened the way toward our definitive fullness. St. Paul expresses it this way: «You have put on the new man, who is being renewed in knowledge after the image of his creator» (Col 3:10; cf. Eph 4:24) (CCC 1709).

St. John Paul II devoted much of his pontificate to developing this truth in his Theology of the Body. His Wednesday catecheses (1979-1984) explore the meaning of the human body as a manifestation of the person. The body is not something we have; it is something we are. And human love — especially in marriage — is a living image of God's love. As he wrote in his first encyclical Redemptor Hominis: «Man cannot live without love. He remains a being that is incomprehensible for himself, his life is senseless, if love is not revealed to him, if he does not encounter love, if he does not experience it and make it his own, if he does not participate intimately in it.» Human dignity is fully realized in love, because love is the fundamental vocation of every human being.

Connection

We live in a culture that measures the value of persons by utilitarian criteria: What do you produce? How much do you earn? Are you useful? This mentality, which Pope Francis has called the «throwaway culture,» inevitably leads to contempt for those who are not «useful»: the unborn, the elderly, the terminally ill, persons with disabilities, the unproductive poor.

Christianity responds with a radically opposite logic, which Pope Emeritus Benedict XVI called the «logic of gift»: a person's worth comes not from what they produce but from being a gift of God. Every human life, however fragile or limited it may appear, is an unrepeatable gift. Jean Vanier, founder of L'Arche communities where people with and without disabilities live together, used to say: «People with disabilities are not a problem to be solved; they are a mystery to be welcomed. They have taught me more about what it means to be human than all my philosophy studies.»

Question: Do you treat people according to their usefulness or according to their dignity? Which people in your life are «invisible» to you because they have nothing to offer you?

3. Dignity as the Foundation of Catholic Social Teaching

The affirmation of human dignity does not remain a beautiful idea; it has practical consequences that transform society. The whole of the Church's social doctrine rests on this principle, and the Catechism gathers it in this introductory section because everything else flows from here (CCC 1700, 1710).

First, radical equality. If we are all made in the image of God, there are no first-class and second-class persons. St. Paul proclaimed it with a revolutionary force for his time and for ours: «There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free, there is neither male nor female; for you are all one in Christ Jesus» (Gal 3:28). No difference — race, nationality, social class, intellectual ability, religion — can erase the fundamental dignity we share. This truth condemns racism, classism, xenophobia, and every form of discrimination that treats anyone as less than a person.

Second, the inviolability of every human life. If every person is the image of God from the moment of conception, then no one has the right to dispose of an innocent human life. Abortion, euthanasia, homicide, assisted suicide: all these practices violate the dignity God has bestowed on the person. They are not simply «moral mistakes»; they are direct attacks against the image of God in the world. The Catechism will state this explicitly in its treatment of the fifth commandment (CCC 2258 ff.), but already in this section it establishes the principle: human life is sacred because God is its author (CCC 1711).

Third, the preferential option for the poor. If dignity is equal in all, then those who suffer most — the poor, the marginalized, the oppressed — deserve special attention. Not because they are more dignified than others, but because their dignity is being trampled and they need someone to defend it. Jesus himself identified with them: «I was hungry and you gave me food, I was thirsty and you gave me drink… as you did it to one of the least of these my brothers, you did it to me» (Mt 25:35-40). Mother Teresa of Calcutta made this principle her whole life: she saw Christ in every person lying in the street, in every abandoned dying person. «The most terrible poverty,» she said, «is not the lack of bread, but the feeling of being unloved, unwanted, abandoned by everyone.»

Fourth, the common good. The dignity of every person demands that social structures — laws, economy, politics — be at the service of the person and not the other way around. No ideology, economic system, or political party can stand above the human person. The State exists to serve persons, not persons to serve the State (CCC 1905-1912).

Modern Errors Against Human Dignity

The Catechism, without naming them directly, provides the tools to unmask the main contemporary attacks on human dignity:

  • Utilitarianism: judges people by their usefulness. If you produce, you are valuable; if you do not, you are a burden. This is the logic underlying eugenic abortion («this child will have a difficult life»), euthanasia («this elderly person only generates costs»), and the discarding of the disabled.
  • Relativism: denies that there is any truth about the human being. If there is no human nature with intrinsic dignity, then dignity becomes something each person defines for themselves, and human rights lose their objective foundation.
  • «Quality of life» arguments: measure the value of a life by external criteria (health, abilities, pleasure). A life with suffering or limitations would be «less valuable» than a healthy and pleasurable life. This directly contradicts the dignity God bestows on every human life, without conditions.
  • Transhumanism: dreams of overcoming human limits through technology, as if being human were a problem to be solved. Christian dignity teaches us that our humanity — with its limits, frailties, and dependencies — is not a defect, but the path by which God meets us and saves us.

Going Deeper

The relationship between human dignity and human rights has been the subject of intense debate in contemporary Catholic thought. The United States Declaration of Independence (1776) affirms that «all men are created equal and are endowed by their Creator with certain unalienable rights.» However, modern secular thought has attempted to ground human rights without reference to God, which raises a serious philosophical problem: if there is no Creator who bestows dignity, what prevents the majority from deciding that certain lives have no value?

Jacques Maritain, the French Catholic philosopher who participated in the drafting of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948), argued that human rights can only be solidly grounded in natural law and, ultimately, in God. His book The Rights of Man and Natural Law (1943) remains an indispensable reference. Pope Francis, in his encyclical Fratelli Tutti (2020), likewise warns that «without the awareness that every human being possesses a unique and inviolable dignity, the discourse of human rights is left exposed to manipulation by those who hold more power.»

Recommended reading: Catechism 1700-1715; YouCat 270-275; St. John Paul II, Centesimus Annus (1991), chapter II: the human person between the State and the market. Also the chapter on the dignity of the person in Compendium of the Social Doctrine of the Church (Pontifical Council for Justice and Peace, 2004).

4. Connection with Scripture: The Psalm of Dignity

The Old Testament contains one of the most beautiful texts on human dignity in Psalm 8:

When I look at your heavens, the work of your fingers,
the moon and the stars which you have established:
what is man that you are mindful of him,
and the son of man that you care for him?
Yet you have made him a little lower than the angels,
and you have crowned him with glory and honor.
You have given him dominion over the works of your hands;
you have put all things under his feet.

— Psalm 8:3-6

The psalmist looks at the starry sky and feels his smallness. It is the same experience we have all had at some point: faced with the immensity of the universe, what importance do I have? But the answer of Scripture is astonishing: God not only remembers us, but has crowned us with glory and honor. We are small in size but immense in value.

And St. Paul, in the letter to the Colossians, takes this truth even further:

«He is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation; for in him all things were created.» (Col 1:15-16)

Christ is the perfect image of God. We are created in the image of God, but Christ is the image of God. And when we are united to Christ by faith and baptism, we are transformed into that same image: «You have put on the new man, who is being renewed in knowledge after the image of his creator» (Col 3:10). The dignity of the Christian is even greater than that of the first Adam: we are not only the image of God, but sons in the Son, heirs of eternal life (CCC 1709).

St. John sums it up with a promise that overwhelms: «Beloved, we are God's children now; it does not yet appear what we shall be, but we know that when he appears we shall be like him, for we shall see him as he is» (1 Jn 3:2). Our present dignity is only the beginning. What we shall be surpasses everything we can imagine.

5. Living According to Your Dignity

St. Leo the Great, Pope of the fifth century, preached to the newly baptized with these words that still resonate: «Christian, recognize your dignity. Now that you have become a partaker of the divine nature, do not return to your former baseness.» Knowing our dignity is not a self-help exercise; it is a call to transformation. If we truly believe that we are made in the image of God and that in Christ we have been made his children, then our life must reflect that reality (CCC 1709-1710).

What does this mean in practice?

Respect your own body. «Do you not know that your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit?» (1 Cor 6:19). The body is not a toy or a tool. It is sacred. Caring for it, respecting it, and using it according to God's plan is an act of faith in the dignity God has given us.

Seek the truth and live in it. We cannot live according to our dignity if we settle for lies, half-truths, or superficiality. The human person, endowed with intelligence, is called to seek the truth and adhere to it with one's whole being.

Treat others with the respect they deserve as persons. Every person you meet today — the cashier at the supermarket, the difficult coworker, the stranger on public transit — is the image of God. Treating them as such is not optional; it is a duty of justice. And it is also the measure of our own faith.

Defend the most vulnerable. Dignity is not something we defend only for ourselves. It is a truth that demands to be defended wherever it is being trampled: in the mother's womb, in the nursing home, at the border, in the prison, in the hospital, in war-torn countries.

The Catechism closes this section with an affirmation of hope (CCC 1710-1715): our dignity is not only a starting point but a destination. We are called to eternal beatitude, to full communion with God. The whole Christian life is a journey toward that fullness. Every act of love, every choice of the good, every renunciation of evil, draws us closer to that goal. Dignity is not only received; it is also built, with God's grace, throughout an entire lifetime.

Activity

This week, whenever you encounter someone (especially someone you normally ignore or look down on), say inwardly: «This person is the image of God.» Notice what changes in you when you do this.

Blessed Carlo Acutis (1991-2006), a teenager who died of leukemia at fifteen, lived this truth with extraordinary naturalness. He said: «Everyone is born as an original, but many die as photocopies.» He understood that his dignity was not about being like everyone else, but about being the unique person God created him to be. His Eucharistic devotion sprang from this certainty: the same God who gave him dignity in creation came to meet him in the Eucharist. Bl. Carlo was beatified in 2020 and is a powerful intercessor for young people struggling with identity.

For Reflection

1. Where does your sense of personal worth really come from?

Be honest. From your work, your relationships, your appearance, social media likes, or from knowing that you are the image of God? What would happen to your sense of worth if you lost your job or your health tomorrow?

2. Is there someone you find it difficult to recognize as «the image of God»?

It could be someone who has hurt you, someone you despise for their political views, someone you mock or ignore. What would change in that relationship if you could see in that person the image of God?

3. Original sin wounded our dignity but did not destroy it. Where do you feel that wound most strongly?

The wound of original sin manifests as interior division, inclination toward evil, difficulty in doing good. In what area of your life (thoughts, words, relationships, habits) do you experience that struggle?

4. «Christ reveals man to man himself» (Gaudium et Spes 22). What do you learn about yourself when you look at Jesus?

Jesus is perfectly free, perfectly self-giving, perfectly merciful. What does that tell you about who you are called to be?

5. If you truly believed that every person you meet is the image of God, what would change in how you treat people this week?

Think of someone concrete. What could you do differently tomorrow?

Key Idea

Key Idea: Your dignity does not depend on what you do, what you have, or what others think of you. God created you in his image, and that is a value no one can take away. Live according to that truth, and defend the dignity of all, especially the most vulnerable.

Cultural Treasures

Film Wonder (Stephen Chbosky, 2017). The story of Auggie Pullman, a boy with a severe facial deformity who enters school for the first time. The film is a moving meditation on what really makes a person valuable. The line «choose kind» captures its message: dignity is recognized in love, not in appearance. Pair it with the original book by R.J. Palacio.

Witness St. Josephine Bakhita (1869-1947). Kidnapped at age seven, sold into slavery, brutally tortured. Her story is the most powerful testimony that human dignity is indestructible. When she encountered Christ, she discovered that her worth had never depended on her captors: «I am definitively loved, and whatever happens to me — I am awaited by this Love. And so my life is good.» Her autobiography is brief but shattering. She was canonized in the year 2000 by St. John Paul II. February 8 is her feast day and the Church's International Day of Prayer and Awareness Against Human Trafficking.

Witness Jean Vanier and L'Arche. Communities founded in 1964 where persons with and without disabilities share life together. Vanier, a philosopher and son of the Governor General of Canada, discovered that the weakest have something to teach the strongest. His book Community and Growth is a classic.

Magisterium St. John Paul II, Theology of the Body (Wednesday catecheses, 1979-1984). The complete cycle of 129 catecheses on human love, the body, and the dignity of the person. Essential reading for understanding contemporary Catholic anthropology. Available in accessible summaries such as Theology of the Body for Beginners by Christopher West.

Magisterium Pope Francis, Fratelli Tutti (2020). The encyclical on fraternity and social friendship devotes extensive space to human dignity as the foundation of a culture of encounter. Chapters I and II are especially relevant to this lesson.

Testimony Blessed Carlo Acutis (1991-2006). An Italian teenager passionate about the Eucharist and technology, who at fifteen faced leukemia with extraordinary peace: «I offer all the suffering I will have to undergo to the Lord, for the Pope and for the Church, so as not to go to Purgatory but to go straight to Heaven.» He knew his dignity came from being loved by God, and he lived that with the naturalness of someone who is completely at ease with who he is. His tomb in Assisi has become a place of pilgrimage for young people. He was beatified in 2020.

Art The Creation of Adam (Michelangelo, 1511). The Sistine Chapel fresco visually captures the moment when God communicates his dignity to the human being. The fingers of God and Adam about to touch: human dignity is born from that contact with the Creator. Also Fra Angelico's frescoes at San Marco in Florence: his figures radiate the dignity of those who know they are loved by God.

Music Hakuna Group Music and their song «Eres Tú» («It Is You»). Hakuna's music expresses the experience of knowing oneself unconditionally loved by God. Their fresh and contemporary style connects especially with young people. Available on Spotify, YouTube, and hakunagroup.com.
